g.data-narration: Welcome to NoSQL Key-Value Stores. Today we explore the simplest yet most powerful NoSQL database model used in modern cloud applications.
g.text: A Comprehensive Guide for university Students

g.data-narration: A key-value store is a database that stores data as unique key-value pairs, where each key uniquely identifies its corresponding value like a dictionary.
g.text: A NoSQL database that stores data as unique key-value pairs

g.data-narration: Unlike relational databases with tables and SQL, key-value stores offer simple GET/SET operations with flexible schemas and horizontal scalability.
The core operations in key-value stores are simple: SET stores a value, GET retrieves it, DELETE removes it, and UPDATE modifies existing data.
Key-value stores offer tunable consistency: strong consistency ensures immediate updates, while eventual consistency trades speed for availability.
g.text: • All reads see most recent write

g.data-narration: The CAP theorem states distributed databases can guarantee only two of three: Consistency, Availability, or Partition Tolerance.
Hash partitioning evenly distributes keys but prevents range queries. Range partitioning enables efficient range queries but may cause hot spots.
Data is replicated across multiple nodes for fault tolerance. Synchronous replication waits for all replicas; asynchronous is faster but may lose data.
In-memory key-value stores like Redis provide extremely low latency by storing data in RAM, ideal for caching and session management.
TTL allows automatic key expiration after a specified duration, making key-value stores perfect for caching and temporary data storage.
Redis is an open-source in-memory store supporting strings, lists, sets, hashes, and sorted sets with optional persistence via RDB and AOF.
Amazon DynamoDB is a fully managed NoSQL service with single-digit millisecond latency, supporting both key-value and document data models.
